g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Cloud and Grid Computing platforms - two transformative paradigms in distributed computing.
Grid Computing coordinates geographically distributed resources across organizations to solve large computational problems.
Cloud Computing provides on-demand IT resources as a service, enabling rapid provisioning with minimal management effort.
Grid platforms feature distributed ownership across multiple organizations and resource sharing as a core principle.
Grid computing uses decentralized federated control and OGSA standards for secure, interoperable resource management.
g.text: Open standards for interoperability | GSI for security

g.data-narration: Grid computing focuses on high-throughput batch processing where thousands of independent computational jobs are scheduled and executed.
g.text: Maximize CPU-years per month | Thousands of independent jobs

g.data-narration: The five essential NIST characteristics of cloud computing are BRNOE: Broad network access, Resource pooling, and Measured service.
g.text: Available over network via standard mechanisms

g.data-narration: The remaining cloud characteristics are On-demand self-service and Rapid elasticity - enabling users to provision resources instantly.
g.text: Scale out and in rapidly | Core cloud feature

g.data-narration: Cloud offers three service models: IaaS provides virtual infrastructure, PaaS offers development platforms, and SaaS delivers ready-to-use applications.
g.text: Gmail, Salesforce | User uses, provider manages all
AWS Beanstalk, Heroku | User deploys, provider manages runtime
AWS EC2, Azure VMs | User manages OS+, provider handles physical

g.data-narration: Cloud deployment models include public clouds for general users, private for single organizations, and hybrid for combining both.
Grid computing focuses on collaborative problem-solving while cloud computing delivers IT as a utility service with commercial models.
Modern platforms blur boundaries: cloud bursting handles peak loads, HPC clouds run traditional grid applications, and data-intensive grids use cloud storage.
Key grid platforms include LHC Computing Grid and Open Science Grid, while leading cloud providers are AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform.
g.text: LHC Computing Grid | Open Science Grid | Earth System Grid

g.data-narration: Remember: Grid = collaborative problem-solving, Cloud = IT utility service. The five NIST characteristics form the foundation of cloud definition.
g.text: • NIST 5: BRNOE - Broad access, Resource pooling,

g.data-narration: For exams, remember the BRNOE acronym for cloud characteristics and focus on comparing ownership, access, and business models between platforms.
g.text: Understanding both paradigms enables better platform selection for modern computing scenarios
